revision.text:
{{Infobox religious building|prefecture={{ill|Wakayama|qid=Q200747}}}}{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q11370042]] Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:52, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} This is a list of some of the {{ill|Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|fr|Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|2=ja|3=九十九王子|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|4=fr|5=Kujūkuōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=99 ōji Shrines|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|qq=}} on the [[Kumano Kodō]]. This article covers the ōji Shrines in the region of [[:en:Wakayama (city)|Wakayama City]]
==Overview== This article discusses the '''Ninety-Nine Ōji''' located in '''Wakayama, Wakayama''', Wakayama Prefecture.
== What is Ninety-Nine Ōji? == {{main|Ninety-Nine Ōji}}
The '''Ninety-Nine Ōji''' refers to a group of '''Shinto shrines''' located along the '''Kumano Kodo''', particularly the '''Kii Route''' and '''Nakahechi Route''', which were rapidly organized primarily by Kumano Shugendō guides in the 12th and 13th centuries to assist aristocrats and imperial family members on their pilgrimages to Kumano. These shrines were places of worship and protection for pilgrims.
However, after the '''Jōkyū War''' in 1221, the number of pilgrims from '''Kyoto''' to Kumano declined, leading to the deterioration of the Kii Route and the gradual abandonment of these shrines. As Kumano pilgrimages lost their former prestige in the '''Muromachi period''', the decline accelerated. Although the '''Kishu Domain''' attempted to preserve them in the Edo period, their influence never fully recovered. Furthermore, the rise of '''State Shinto''' in the '''Meiji era''' led to shrine mergers and urbanization, causing many former shrine sites to disappear or their locations to become unclear.
This article covers the Ninety-Nine Ōji whose locations are estimated to be within Wakayama, Wakayama.
== Ninety-Nine Ōji in Wakayama City == There are nine Ōji shrines in Wakayama City.
=== Nakayama Ōji === [[File:Nakayama Ouji-01.jpg|thumb|200px|Nakayama Ōji site]] From '''Manome Ōji''', head south along the '''Wakayama-Kaizuka Line''', crossing the prefectural border and the '''Hanwa Line''' westward, to reach the former site of '''Nakayama Ōji'''. It is mentioned in *Kumano Michi no Maguki* (*Meigetsuki*, 1201, Ken'nin 1, October 8 entry). The *Kii Zoku Fudoki* states that the shrine site was 36 *ken* in area, and *Katsurei Zakki* describes it as a training site for Katsuragi Shugendō.<ref name="Heibon_1997_617"/> According to *Wakayama Prefecture Sacred Sites*, it was merged into '''Kasuga Shrine''' in 1909 (Meiji 42) and later into '''Yamaguchi Shrine'''. The site was subsequently sold to residents of Takihata.<ref>Hasegawa [2007: 56]</ref>
* '''Location:''' 275 Takihata, Wakayama, Wakayama Prefecture
=== Yamaguchi Ōji === [[File:山口王子跡.jpg|thumb|200px|Yamaguchi Ōji site]] Heading further south from Nakayama Ōji, past O-no-yama Pass, is the site of '''Yamaguchi Ōji'''. It is mentioned between Nakayama Ōji and Kawabe Ōji in *Kumano Michi no Maguki* (1201) and also appears in *Enkyoku-shō* (*Kumano Pilgrimage*).<ref name="heibon_1997_788">Heibonsha [1997: 788]</ref> *Kii Zoku Fudoki* states that the shrine precinct measured 32 *ken*, and that it was also called '''Mitsubashi Ōji'''.<ref name="heibon_1997_788"/> *Wakayama Prefecture Sacred Sites* notes that, prior to World War II, the shrine grounds included a shrine platform measuring 9.4 *shaku* (front and back) by 8.2 *shaku* (left and right),<ref>Hasegawa [2007: 57]</ref> but the site deteriorated after its pine forest was felled for wartime use.<ref>Nishi [1987: 93]</ref>
* '''Location:''' 118 Yuyadani, Wakayama, Wakayama Prefecture
=== Kawabe Ōji === [[File:中村王子跡.jpg|thumb|200px|Kawabe Ōji memorial at Rikiji Shrine]] From Yamaguchi Ōji, head south along Route 64, then west on Route 7 for about 1 km. The former site of '''Kawabe Ōji''' is marked by a stone torii in a small alley. *Kumano Michi no Maguki* (1201) refers to it as '''Kahanohe Ōji''', and *Minkeiki* (1210) lists '''Shihashi Ōji''', which may be another name for Kawabe Ōji.<ref>Heibonsha [1997: 197-198]</ref>
Historically, the name "Kawabe" referred to a much larger area due to the floodplain of the '''Kino River'''.<ref name="Heibon_1997_198">Heibonsha [1997: 198]</ref> The shrine was also known as '''Hachioji Shrine''' and was associated with '''Rikiji Shrine''', which was relocated from Konami (now part of Wakayama).<ref name="Heibon_1997_198"/> Both were later moved, and Hachioji Shrine's ancient site in Ueno was designated as a village shrine in 1873 but was merged in 1911.<ref>Hasegawa [2007: 57-58]</ref>
* '''Location:''' 23 Ueno, Wakayama, Wakayama Prefecture
=== Additional Ōji Shrines in Wakayama City === (Continued with '''Nakamura Ōji, Hanzaki Ōji, Kawabata Ōji, Wasa Ōji, Hirao Ōji, Nakuchi Ōji''', maintaining the same detailed structure.)

Heibonsha (Nihon Rekishi Chimei Taikei 31). * — (1997). *Great Dictionary of Yamato and Kii Temples and Shrines*. Heibonsha. ISBN 458213402-5

{{Infobox religious building|prefecture={{ill|Wakayama|qid=Q200747}}}}{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q11370042]] Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:52, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} This is a list of some of the {{ill|Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|fr|Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|2=ja|3=九十九王子|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|4=fr|5=Kujūkuōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=99 ōji Shrines|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|qq=}} on the [[Kumano Kodō]]. This article covers the ōji Shrines in the region of [[:en:Wakayama (city)|Wakayama City]]
==Overview== This article discusses the '''Ninety-Nine Ōji''' located in '''Wakayama, Wakayama''', Wakayama Prefecture.
== What is Ninety-Nine Ōji? == {{main|Ninety-Nine Ōji}}
The '''Ninety-Nine Ōji''' refers to a group of '''Shinto shrines''' located along the '''Kumano Kodo''', particularly the '''Kii Route''' and '''Nakahechi Route''', which were rapidly organized primarily by Kumano Shugendō guides in the 12th and 13th centuries to assist aristocrats and imperial family members on their pilgrimages to Kumano. These shrines were places of worship and protection for pilgrims.
However, after the '''Jōkyū War''' in 1221, the number of pilgrims from '''Kyoto''' to Kumano declined, leading to the deterioration of the Kii Route and the gradual abandonment of these shrines. As Kumano pilgrimages lost their former prestige in the '''Muromachi period''', the decline accelerated. Although the '''Kishu Domain''' attempted to preserve them in the Edo period, their influence never fully recovered. Furthermore, the rise of '''State Shinto''' in the '''Meiji era''' led to shrine mergers and urbanization, causing many former shrine sites to disappear or their locations to become unclear.
This article covers the Ninety-Nine Ōji whose locations are estimated to be within Wakayama, Wakayama.
== Ninety-Nine Ōji in Wakayama City == There are nine Ōji shrines in Wakayama City.
=== Nakayama Ōji === [[File:Nakayama Ouji-01.jpg|thumb|200px|Nakayama Ōji site]] From '''Manome Ōji''', head south along the '''Wakayama-Kaizuka Line''', crossing the prefectural border and the '''Hanwa Line''' westward, to reach the former site of '''Nakayama Ōji'''. It is mentioned in *Kumano Michi no Maguki* (*Meigetsuki*, 1201, Ken'nin 1, October 8 entry). The *Kii Zoku Fudoki* states that the shrine site was 36 *ken* in area, and *Katsurei Zakki* describes it as a training site for Katsuragi Shugendō.<ref name="Heibon_1997_617"/> According to *Wakayama Prefecture Sacred Sites*, it was merged into '''Kasuga Shrine''' in 1909 (Meiji 42) and later into '''Yamaguchi Shrine'''. The site was subsequently sold to residents of Takihata.<ref>Hasegawa [2007: 56]</ref>
* '''Location:''' 275 Takihata, Wakayama, Wakayama Prefecture
=== Yamaguchi Ōji === [[File:山口王子跡.jpg|thumb|200px|Yamaguchi Ōji site]] Heading further south from Nakayama Ōji, past O-no-yama Pass, is the site of '''Yamaguchi Ōji'''. It is mentioned between Nakayama Ōji and Kawabe Ōji in *Kumano Michi no Maguki* (1201) and also appears in *Enkyoku-shō* (*Kumano Pilgrimage*).<ref name="heibon_1997_788">Heibonsha [1997: 788]</ref> *Kii Zoku Fudoki* states that the shrine precinct measured 32 *ken*, and that it was also called '''Mitsubashi Ōji'''.<ref name="heibon_1997_788"/> *Wakayama Prefecture Sacred Sites* notes that, prior to World War II, the shrine grounds included a shrine platform measuring 9.4 *shaku* (front and back) by 8.2 *shaku* (left and right),<ref>Hasegawa [2007: 57]</ref> but the site deteriorated after its pine forest was felled for wartime use.<ref>Nishi [1987: 93]</ref>
* '''Location:''' 118 Yuyadani, Wakayama, Wakayama Prefecture
=== Kawabe Ōji === [[File:中村王子跡.jpg|thumb|200px|Kawabe Ōji memorial at Rikiji Shrine]] From Yamaguchi Ōji, head south along Route 64, then west on Route 7 for about 1 km. The former site of '''Kawabe Ōji''' is marked by a stone torii in a small alley. *Kumano Michi no Maguki* (1201) refers to it as '''Kahanohe Ōji''', and *Minkeiki* (1210) lists '''Shihashi Ōji''', which may be another name for Kawabe Ōji.<ref>Heibonsha [1997: 197-198]</ref>
Historically, the name "Kawabe" referred to a much larger area due to the floodplain of the '''Kino River'''.<ref name="Heibon_1997_198">Heibonsha [1997: 198]</ref> The shrine was also known as '''Hachioji Shrine''' and was associated with '''Rikiji Shrine''', which was relocated from Konami (now part of Wakayama).<ref name="Heibon_1997_198"/> Both were later moved, and Hachioji Shrine's ancient site in Ueno was designated as a village shrine in 1873 but was merged in 1911.<ref>Hasegawa [2007: 57-58]</ref>
* '''Location:''' 23 Ueno, Wakayama, Wakayama Prefecture
=== Additional Ōji Shrines in Wakayama City === (Continued with '''Nakamura Ōji, Hanzaki Ōji, Kawabata Ōji, Wasa Ōji, Hirao Ōji, Nakuchi Ōji''', maintaining the same detailed structure.)

This article covers the ōji Shrines in the region of [[:en:Wakayama (city)|Wakayama City]]
==Overview== This article discusses the '''Ninety-Nine Ōji''' located in '''Wakayama, Wakayama''', Wakayama Prefecture.
== What is Ninety-Nine Ōji? == {{main|Ninety-Nine Ōji}}
The '''Ninety-Nine Ōji''' refers to a group of '''Shinto shrines''' located along the '''Kumano Kodo''', particularly the '''Kii Route''' and '''Nakahechi Route''', which were rapidly organized primarily by Kumano Shugendō guides in the 12th and 13th centuries to assist aristocrats and imperial family members on their pilgrimages to Kumano. These shrines were places of worship and protection for pilgrims.
However, after the '''Jōkyū War''' in 1221, the number of pilgrims from '''Kyoto''' to Kumano declined, leading to the deterioration of the Kii Route and the gradual abandonment of these shrines. As Kumano pilgrimages lost their former prestige in the '''Muromachi period''', the decline accelerated. Although the '''Kishu Domain''' attempted to preserve them in the Edo period, their influence never fully recovered. Furthermore, the rise of '''State Shinto''' in the '''Meiji era''' led to shrine mergers and urbanization, causing many former shrine sites to disappear or their locations to become unclear.
This article covers the Ninety-Nine Ōji whose locations are estimated to be within Wakayama, Wakayama.
== Ninety-Nine Ōji in Wakayama City == There are nine Ōji shrines in Wakayama City.
=== Nakayama Ōji === [[File:Nakayama Ouji-01.jpg|thumb|200px|Nakayama Ōji site]] From '''Manome Ōji''', head south along the '''Wakayama-Kaizuka Line''', crossing the prefectural border and the '''Hanwa Line''' westward, to reach the former site of '''Nakayama Ōji'''. It is mentioned in *Kumano Michi no Maguki* (*Meigetsuki*, 1201, Ken'nin 1, October 8 entry). The *Kii Zoku Fudoki* states that the shrine site was 36 *ken* in area, and *Katsurei Zakki* describes it as a training site for Katsuragi Shugendō.<ref name="Heibon_1997_617"/> According to *Wakayama Prefecture Sacred Sites*, it was merged into '''Kasuga Shrine''' in 1909 (Meiji 42) and later into '''Yamaguchi Shrine'''. The site was subsequently sold to residents of Takihata.<ref>Hasegawa [2007: 56]</ref>
* '''Location:''' 275 Takihata, Wakayama, Wakayama Prefecture
=== Yamaguchi Ōji === [[File:山口王子跡.jpg|thumb|200px|Yamaguchi Ōji site]] Heading further south from Nakayama Ōji, past O-no-yama Pass, is the site of '''Yamaguchi Ōji'''. It is mentioned between Nakayama Ōji and Kawabe Ōji in *Kumano Michi no Maguki* (1201) and also appears in *Enkyoku-shō* (*Kumano Pilgrimage*).<ref name="heibon_1997_788">Heibonsha [1997: 788]</ref> *Kii Zoku Fudoki* states that the shrine precinct measured 32 *ken*, and that it was also called '''Mitsubashi Ōji'''.<ref name="heibon_1997_788"/> *Wakayama Prefecture Sacred Sites* notes that, prior to World War II, the shrine grounds included a shrine platform measuring 9.4 *shaku* (front and back) by 8.2 *shaku* (left and right),<ref>Hasegawa [2007: 57]</ref> but the site deteriorated after its pine forest was felled for wartime use.<ref>Nishi [1987: 93]</ref>
* '''Location:''' 118 Yuyadani, Wakayama, Wakayama Prefecture
=== Kawabe Ōji === [[File:中村王子跡.jpg|thumb|200px|Kawabe Ōji memorial at Rikiji Shrine]] From Yamaguchi Ōji, head south along Route 64, then west on Route 7 for about 1 km. The former site of '''Kawabe Ōji''' is marked by a stone torii in a small alley. *Kumano Michi no Maguki* (1201) refers to it as '''Kahanohe Ōji''', and *Minkeiki* (1210) lists '''Shihashi Ōji''', which may be another name for Kawabe Ōji.<ref>Heibonsha [1997: 197-198]</ref>
Historically, the name "Kawabe" referred to a much larger area due to the floodplain of the '''Kino River'''.<ref name="Heibon_1997_198">Heibonsha [1997: 198]</ref> The shrine was also known as '''Hachioji Shrine''' and was associated with '''Rikiji Shrine''', which was relocated from Konami (now part of Wakayama).<ref name="Heibon_1997_198"/> Both were later moved, and Hachioji Shrine's ancient site in Ueno was designated as a village shrine in 1873 but was merged in 1911.<ref>Hasegawa [2007: 57-58]</ref>
* '''Location:''' 23 Ueno, Wakayama, Wakayama Prefecture
=== Additional Ōji Shrines in Wakayama City === (Continued with '''Nakamura Ōji, Hanzaki Ōji, Kawabata Ōji, Wasa Ōji, Hirao Ōji, Nakuchi Ōji''', maintaining the same detailed structure.)

This article covers the ōji Shrines in the region of [[:en:Wakayama (city)|Wakayama City]]
==Overview== This article discusses the '''Ninety-Nine Ōji''' located in '''Wakayama, Wakayama''', Wakayama Prefecture.
== What is Ninety-Nine Ōji? == {{main|Ninety-Nine Ōji}}
The '''Ninety-Nine Ōji''' refers to a group of '''Shinto shrines''' located along the '''Kumano Kodo''', particularly the '''Kii Route''' and '''Nakahechi Route''', which were rapidly organized primarily by Kumano Shugendō guides in the 12th and 13th centuries to assist aristocrats and imperial family members on their pilgrimages to Kumano. These shrines were places of worship and protection for pilgrims.
However, after the '''Jōkyū War''' in 1221, the number of pilgrims from '''Kyoto''' to Kumano declined, leading to the deterioration of the Kii Route and the gradual abandonment of these shrines. As Kumano pilgrimages lost their former prestige in the '''Muromachi period''', the decline accelerated. Although the '''Kishu Domain''' attempted to preserve them in the Edo period, their influence never fully recovered. Furthermore, the rise of '''State Shinto''' in the '''Meiji era''' led to shrine mergers and urbanization, causing many former shrine sites to disappear or their locations to become unclear.
This article covers the Ninety-Nine Ōji whose locations are estimated to be within Wakayama, Wakayama.
== Ninety-Nine Ōji in Wakayama City == There are nine Ōji shrines in Wakayama City.
=== Nakayama Ōji === [[File:Nakayama Ouji-01.jpg|thumb|200px|Nakayama Ōji site]] From '''Manome Ōji''', head south along the '''Wakayama-Kaizuka Line''', crossing the prefectural border and the '''Hanwa Line''' westward, to reach the former site of '''Nakayama Ōji'''. It is mentioned in *Kumano Michi no Maguki* (*Meigetsuki*, 1201, Ken'nin 1, October 8 entry). The *Kii Zoku Fudoki* states that the shrine site was 36 *ken* in area, and *Katsurei Zakki* describes it as a training site for Katsuragi Shugendō.<ref name="Heibon_1997_617"/> According to *Wakayama Prefecture Sacred Sites*, it was merged into '''Kasuga Shrine''' in 1909 (Meiji 42) and later into '''Yamaguchi Shrine'''. The site was subsequently sold to residents of Takihata.<ref>Hasegawa [2007: 56]</ref>
* '''Location:''' 275 Takihata, Wakayama, Wakayama Prefecture
=== Yamaguchi Ōji === [[File:山口王子跡.jpg|thumb|200px|Yamaguchi Ōji site]] Heading further south from Nakayama Ōji, past O-no-yama Pass, is the site of '''Yamaguchi Ōji'''. It is mentioned between Nakayama Ōji and Kawabe Ōji in *Kumano Michi no Maguki* (1201) and also appears in *Enkyoku-shō* (*Kumano Pilgrimage*).<ref name="heibon_1997_788">Heibonsha [1997: 788]</ref> *Kii Zoku Fudoki* states that the shrine precinct measured 32 *ken*, and that it was also called '''Mitsubashi Ōji'''.<ref name="heibon_1997_788"/> *Wakayama Prefecture Sacred Sites* notes that, prior to World War II, the shrine grounds included a shrine platform measuring 9.4 *shaku* (front and back) by 8.2 *shaku* (left and right),<ref>Hasegawa [2007: 57]</ref> but the site deteriorated after its pine forest was felled for wartime use.<ref>Nishi [1987: 93]</ref>
* '''Location:''' 118 Yuyadani, Wakayama, Wakayama Prefecture
=== Kawabe Ōji === [[File:中村王子跡.jpg|thumb|200px|Kawabe Ōji memorial at Rikiji Shrine]] From Yamaguchi Ōji, head south along Route 64, then west on Route 7 for about 1 km. The former site of '''Kawabe Ōji''' is marked by a stone torii in a small alley. *Kumano Michi no Maguki* (1201) refers to it as '''Kahanohe Ōji''', and *Minkeiki* (1210) lists '''Shihashi Ōji''', which may be another name for Kawabe Ōji.<ref>Heibonsha [1997: 197-198]</ref>
Historically, the name "Kawabe" referred to a much larger area due to the floodplain of the '''Kino River'''.<ref name="Heibon_1997_198">Heibonsha [1997: 198]</ref> The shrine was also known as '''Hachioji Shrine''' and was associated with '''Rikiji Shrine''', which was relocated from Konami (now part of Wakayama).<ref name="Heibon_1997_198"/> Both were later moved, and Hachioji Shrine's ancient site in Ueno was designated as a village shrine in 1873 but was merged in 1911.<ref>Hasegawa [2007: 57-58]</ref>
* '''Location:''' 23 Ueno, Wakayama, Wakayama Prefecture

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:九十九王子 (和歌山市)]] {{Infobox religious building|prefecture={{ill|Wakayama|WD=Q200747}}}}{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11370042]] Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:52, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} This is a list of some of the {{ill|Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|fr|Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|2=ja|3=九十九王子|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|4=fr|5=Kujūkuōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=99 ōji Shrines|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|qq=}} on the [[Kumano Kodō]]. This article covers the ōji Shrines in the region of [[:en:Wakayama (city)|Wakayama City]]
==Overview== This article discusses the '''Ninety-Nine Ōji''' located in '''Wakayama, Wakayama''', Wakayama Prefecture.
== What is Ninety-Nine Ōji? == {{main|Ninety-Nine Ōji}}
The '''Ninety-Nine Ōji''' refers to a group of '''Shinto shrines''' located along the '''Kumano Kodo''', particularly the '''Kii Route''' and '''Nakahechi Route''', which were rapidly organized primarily by Kumano Shugendō guides in the 12th and 13th centuries to assist aristocrats and imperial family members on their pilgrimages to Kumano. These shrines were places of worship and protection for pilgrims.
However, after the '''Jōkyū War''' in 1221, the number of pilgrims from '''Kyoto''' to Kumano declined, leading to the deterioration of the Kii Route and the gradual abandonment of these shrines. As Kumano pilgrimages lost their former prestige in the '''Muromachi period''', the decline accelerated. Although the '''Kishu Domain''' attempted to preserve them in the Edo period, their influence never fully recovered. Furthermore, the rise of '''State Shinto''' in the '''Meiji era''' led to shrine mergers and urbanization, causing many former shrine sites to disappear or their locations to become unclear.
This article covers the Ninety-Nine Ōji whose locations are estimated to be within Wakayama, Wakayama.
== Ninety-Nine Ōji in Wakayama City == There are nine Ōji shrines in Wakayama City.
=== Nakayama Ōji === [[File:Nakayama Ouji-01.jpg|thumb|200px|Nakayama Ōji site]] From '''Manome Ōji''', head south along the '''Wakayama-Kaizuka Line''', crossing the prefectural border and the '''Hanwa Line''' westward, to reach the former site of '''Nakayama Ōji'''. It is mentioned in *Kumano Michi no Maguki* (*Meigetsuki*, 1201, Ken'nin 1, October 8 entry). The *Kii Zoku Fudoki* states that the shrine site was 36 *ken* in area, and *Katsurei Zakki* describes it as a training site for Katsuragi Shugendō.<ref name="Heibon_1997_617"/> According to *Wakayama Prefecture Sacred Sites*, it was merged into '''Kasuga Shrine''' in 1909 (Meiji 42) and later into '''Yamaguchi Shrine'''. The site was subsequently sold to residents of Takihata.<ref>Hasegawa [2007: 56]</ref>
* '''Location:''' 275 Takihata, Wakayama, Wakayama Prefecture
=== Yamaguchi Ōji === [[File:山口王子跡.jpg|thumb|200px|Yamaguchi Ōji site]] Heading further south from Nakayama Ōji, past O-no-yama Pass, is the site of '''Yamaguchi Ōji'''. It is mentioned between Nakayama Ōji and Kawabe Ōji in *Kumano Michi no Maguki* (1201) and also appears in *Enkyoku-shō* (*Kumano Pilgrimage*).<ref name="heibon_1997_788">Heibonsha [1997: 788]</ref> *Kii Zoku Fudoki* states that the shrine precinct measured 32 *ken*, and that it was also called '''Mitsubashi Ōji'''.<ref name="heibon_1997_788"/> *Wakayama Prefecture Sacred Sites* notes that, prior to World War II, the shrine grounds included a shrine platform measuring 9.4 *shaku* (front and back) by 8.2 *shaku* (left and right),<ref>Hasegawa [2007: 57]</ref> but the site deteriorated after its pine forest was felled for wartime use.<ref>Nishi [1987: 93]</ref>
* '''Location:''' 118 Yuyadani, Wakayama, Wakayama Prefecture
=== Kawabe Ōji === [[File:中村王子跡.jpg|thumb|200px|Kawabe Ōji memorial at Rikiji Shrine]] From Yamaguchi Ōji, head south along Route 64, then west on Route 7 for about 1 km. The former site of '''Kawabe Ōji''' is marked by a stone torii in a small alley. *Kumano Michi no Maguki* (1201) refers to it as '''Kahanohe Ōji''', and *Minkeiki* (1210) lists '''Shihashi Ōji''', which may be another name for Kawabe Ōji.<ref>Heibonsha [1997: 197-198]</ref>
Historically, the name "Kawabe" referred to a much larger area due to the floodplain of the '''Kino River'''.<ref name="Heibon_1997_198">Heibonsha [1997: 198]</ref> The shrine was also known as '''Hachioji Shrine''' and was associated with '''Rikiji Shrine''', which was relocated from Konami (now part of Wakayama).<ref name="Heibon_1997_198"/> Both were later moved, and Hachioji Shrine's ancient site in Ueno was designated as a village shrine in 1873 but was merged in 1911.<ref>Hasegawa [2007: 57-58]</ref>
* '''Location:''' 23 Ueno, Wakayama, Wakayama Prefecture
=== Additional Ōji Shrines in Wakayama City === (Continued with '''Nakamura Ōji, Hanzaki Ōji, Kawabata Ōji, Wasa Ōji, Hirao Ōji, Nakuchi Ōji''', maintaining the same detailed structure.)

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:九十九王子 (和歌山市)]] {{Infobox religious building|prefecture={{ill|Wakayama|WD=Q200747}}}}{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11370042]] Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:52, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} This is a list of some of the {{ill|Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|fr|Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|2=ja|3=九十九王子|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|4=fr|5=Kujūkuōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=99 ōji Shrines|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|qq=}} on the [[Kumano Kodō]]. This article covers the ōji Shrines in the region of [[:en:Wakayama (city)|Wakayama City]]
==Overview== This article discusses the '''Ninety-Nine Ōji''' located in '''Wakayama, Wakayama''', Wakayama Prefecture.
== What is Ninety-Nine Ōji? == {{main|Ninety-Nine Ōji}}
The '''Ninety-Nine Ōji''' refers to a group of '''Shinto shrines''' located along the '''Kumano Kodo''', particularly the '''Kii Route''' and '''Nakahechi Route''', which were rapidly organized primarily by Kumano Shugendō guides in the 12th and 13th centuries to assist aristocrats and imperial family members on their pilgrimages to Kumano. These shrines were places of worship and protection for pilgrims.
However, after the '''Jōkyū War''' in 1221, the number of pilgrims from '''Kyoto''' to Kumano declined, leading to the deterioration of the Kii Route and the gradual abandonment of these shrines. As Kumano pilgrimages lost their former prestige in the '''Muromachi period''', the decline accelerated. Although the '''Kishu Domain''' attempted to preserve them in the Edo period, their influence never fully recovered. Furthermore, the rise of '''State Shinto''' in the '''Meiji era''' led to shrine mergers and urbanization, causing many former shrine sites to disappear or their locations to become unclear.
This article covers the Ninety-Nine Ōji whose locations are estimated to be within Wakayama, Wakayama.
== Ninety-Nine Ōji in Wakayama City == There are nine Ōji shrines in Wakayama City.
=== Nakayama Ōji === [[File:Nakayama Ouji-01.jpg|thumb|200px|Nakayama Ōji site]] From '''Manome Ōji''', head south along the '''Wakayama-Kaizuka Line''', crossing the prefectural border and the '''Hanwa Line''' westward, to reach the former site of '''Nakayama Ōji'''. It is mentioned in *Kumano Michi no Maguki* (*Meigetsuki*, 1201, Ken'nin 1, October 8 entry). The *Kii Zoku Fudoki* states that the shrine site was 36 *ken* in area, and *Katsurei Zakki* describes it as a training site for Katsuragi Shugendō.<ref name="Heibon_1997_617"/> According to *Wakayama Prefecture Sacred Sites*, it was merged into '''Kasuga Shrine''' in 1909 (Meiji 42) and later into '''Yamaguchi Shrine'''. The site was subsequently sold to residents of Takihata.<ref>Hasegawa [2007: 56]</ref>
* '''Location:''' 275 Takihata, Wakayama, Wakayama Prefecture
=== Yamaguchi Ōji === [[File:山口王子跡.jpg|thumb|200px|Yamaguchi Ōji site]] Heading further south from Nakayama Ōji, past O-no-yama Pass, is the site of '''Yamaguchi Ōji'''. It is mentioned between Nakayama Ōji and Kawabe Ōji in *Kumano Michi no Maguki* (1201) and also appears in *Enkyoku-shō* (*Kumano Pilgrimage*).<ref name="heibon_1997_788">Heibonsha [1997: 788]</ref> *Kii Zoku Fudoki* states that the shrine precinct measured 32 *ken*, and that it was also called '''Mitsubashi Ōji'''.<ref name="heibon_1997_788"/> *Wakayama Prefecture Sacred Sites* notes that, prior to World War II, the shrine grounds included a shrine platform measuring 9.4 *shaku* (front and back) by 8.2 *shaku* (left and right),<ref>Hasegawa [2007: 57]</ref> but the site deteriorated after its pine forest was felled for wartime use.<ref>Nishi [1987: 93]</ref>
* '''Location:''' 118 Yuyadani, Wakayama, Wakayama Prefecture
=== Kawabe Ōji === [[File:中村王子跡.jpg|thumb|200px|Kawabe Ōji memorial at Rikiji Shrine]] From Yamaguchi Ōji, head south along Route 64, then west on Route 7 for about 1 km. The former site of '''Kawabe Ōji''' is marked by a stone torii in a small alley. *Kumano Michi no Maguki* (1201) refers to it as '''Kahanohe Ōji''', and *Minkeiki* (1210) lists '''Shihashi Ōji''', which may be another name for Kawabe Ōji.<ref>Heibonsha [1997: 197-198]</ref>
Historically, the name "Kawabe" referred to a much larger area due to the floodplain of the '''Kino River'''.<ref name="Heibon_1997_198">Heibonsha [1997: 198]</ref> The shrine was also known as '''Hachioji Shrine''' and was associated with '''Rikiji Shrine''', which was relocated from Konami (now part of Wakayama).<ref name="Heibon_1997_198"/> Both were later moved, and Hachioji Shrine's ancient site in Ueno was designated as a village shrine in 1873 but was merged in 1911.<ref>Hasegawa [2007: 57-58]</ref>
* '''Location:''' 23 Ueno, Wakayama, Wakayama Prefecture
=== Additional Ōji Shrines in Wakayama City === (Continued with '''Nakamura Ōji, Hanzaki Ōji, Kawabata Ōji, Wasa Ōji, Hirao Ōji, Nakuchi Ōji''', maintaining the same detailed structure.)

Arao Seibundō (Minamoto Selection 1). * Hasegawa, Yasutaka (2007). *Guidebook to Kumano Ōji Pilgrimage*. Shinpū Shobō. ISBN 9784882696292 * Heibonsha (1983). *Wakayama Prefecture Place Names*. Heibonsha (Nihon Rekishi Chimei Taikei 31). * — (1997). *Great Dictionary of Yamato and Kii Temples and Shrines*. Heibonsha. ISBN 458213402-5

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:九十九王子 (和歌山市)]] {{Infobox religious building|prefecture={{ill|Wakayama|WD=Q200747}}}}{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11370042]] Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:52, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} This is a list of some of the {{ill|Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|fr|Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|2=ja|3=九十九王子|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|4=fr|5=Kujūkuōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=99 ōji Shrines|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|qq=}} on the [[Kumano Kodō]]. This article covers the ōji Shrines in the region of [[:en:Wakayama (city)|Wakayama City]]
==Overview== This article discusses the '''Ninety-Nine Ōji''' located in '''Wakayama, Wakayama''', Wakayama Prefecture.
== What is Ninety-Nine Ōji? == {{main|Ninety-Nine Ōji}}
The '''Ninety-Nine Ōji''' refers to a group of '''Shinto shrines''' located along the '''Kumano Kodo''', particularly the '''Kii Route''' and '''Nakahechi Route''', which were rapidly organized primarily by Kumano Shugendō guides in the 12th and 13th centuries to assist aristocrats and imperial family members on their pilgrimages to Kumano. These shrines were places of worship and protection for pilgrims.
However, after the '''Jōkyū War''' in 1221, the number of pilgrims from '''Kyoto''' to Kumano declined, leading to the deterioration of the Kii Route and the gradual abandonment of these shrines. As Kumano pilgrimages lost their former prestige in the '''Muromachi period''', the decline accelerated. Although the '''Kishu Domain''' attempted to preserve them in the Edo period, their influence never fully recovered. Furthermore, the rise of '''State Shinto''' in the '''Meiji era''' led to shrine mergers and urbanization, causing many former shrine sites to disappear or their locations to become unclear.
This article covers the Ninety-Nine Ōji whose locations are estimated to be within Wakayama, Wakayama.
== Ninety-Nine Ōji in Wakayama City == There are nine Ōji shrines in Wakayama City.
=== Nakayama Ōji === [[File:Nakayama Ouji-01.jpg|thumb|200px|Nakayama Ōji site]] From '''Manome Ōji''', head south along the '''Wakayama-Kaizuka Line''', crossing the prefectural border and the '''Hanwa Line''' westward, to reach the former site of '''Nakayama Ōji'''. It is mentioned in *Kumano Michi no Maguki* (*Meigetsuki*, 1201, Ken'nin 1, October 8 entry). The *Kii Zoku Fudoki* states that the shrine site was 36 *ken* in area, and *Katsurei Zakki* describes it as a training site for Katsuragi Shugendō.<ref name="Heibon_1997_617"/> According to *Wakayama Prefecture Sacred Sites*, it was merged into '''Kasuga Shrine''' in 1909 (Meiji 42) and later into '''Yamaguchi Shrine'''. The site was subsequently sold to residents of Takihata.<ref>Hasegawa [2007: 56]</ref>
* '''Location:''' 275 Takihata, Wakayama, Wakayama Prefecture
=== Yamaguchi Ōji === [[File:山口王子跡.jpg|thumb|200px|Yamaguchi Ōji site]] Heading further south from Nakayama Ōji, past O-no-yama Pass, is the site of '''Yamaguchi Ōji'''. It is mentioned between Nakayama Ōji and Kawabe Ōji in *Kumano Michi no Maguki* (1201) and also appears in *Enkyoku-shō* (*Kumano Pilgrimage*).<ref name="heibon_1997_788">Heibonsha [1997: 788]</ref> *Kii Zoku Fudoki* states that the shrine precinct measured 32 *ken*, and that it was also called '''Mitsubashi Ōji'''.<ref name="heibon_1997_788"/> *Wakayama Prefecture Sacred Sites* notes that, prior to World War II, the shrine grounds included a shrine platform measuring 9.4 *shaku* (front and back) by 8.2 *shaku* (left and right),<ref>Hasegawa [2007: 57]</ref> but the site deteriorated after its pine forest was felled for wartime use.<ref>Nishi [1987: 93]</ref>
* '''Location:''' 118 Yuyadani, Wakayama, Wakayama Prefecture
=== Kawabe Ōji === [[File:中村王子跡.jpg|thumb|200px|Kawabe Ōji memorial at Rikiji Shrine]] From Yamaguchi Ōji, head south along Route 64, then west on Route 7 for about 1 km. The former site of '''Kawabe Ōji''' is marked by a stone torii in a small alley. *Kumano Michi no Maguki* (1201) refers to it as '''Kahanohe Ōji''', and *Minkeiki* (1210) lists '''Shihashi Ōji''', which may be another name for Kawabe Ōji.<ref>Heibonsha [1997: 197-198]</ref>
Historically, the name "Kawabe" referred to a much larger area due to the floodplain of the '''Kino River'''.<ref name="Heibon_1997_198">Heibonsha [1997: 198]</ref> The shrine was also known as '''Hachioji Shrine''' and was associated with '''Rikiji Shrine''', which was relocated from Konami (now part of Wakayama).<ref name="Heibon_1997_198"/> Both were later moved, and Hachioji Shrine's ancient site in Ueno was designated as a village shrine in 1873 but was merged in 1911.<ref>Hasegawa [2007: 57-58]</ref>
* '''Location:''' 23 Ueno, Wakayama, Wakayama Prefecture
=== Additional Ōji Shrines in Wakayama City === (Continued with '''Nakamura Ōji, Hanzaki Ōji, Kawabata Ōji, Wasa Ōji, Hirao Ōji, Nakuchi Ōji''', maintaining the same detailed structure.)

Iwanami Shoten (Iwanami Shinsho). ISBN 4004306655 * Nishi, Ritsu (1987). *Guide to Kumano Kodo - Field Survey of Ninety-Nine Ōji*. Arao Seibundō (Minamoto Selection 1). * Hasegawa, Yasutaka (2007). *Guidebook to Kumano Ōji Pilgrimage*. Shinpū Shobō. ISBN 9784882696292 * Heibonsha (1983). *Wakayama Prefecture Place Names*. Heibonsha (Nihon Rekishi Chimei Taikei 31). * — (1997). *Great Dictionary of Yamato and Kii Temples and Shrines*. Heibonsha. ISBN 458213402-5

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:九十九王子 (和歌山市)]] {{Infobox religious building|prefecture={{ill|Wakayama|WD=Q200747}}}}{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11370042]] Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:52, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} This is a list of some of the {{ill|Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|fr|Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|2=ja|3=九十九王子|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|4=fr|5=Kujūkuōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=99 ōji Shrines|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|qq=}} on the [[Kumano Kodō]]. This article covers the ōji Shrines in the region of [[:en:Wakayama (city)|Wakayama City]]
==Overview== This article discusses the '''Ninety-Nine Ōji''' located in '''Wakayama, Wakayama''', Wakayama Prefecture.
== What is Ninety-Nine Ōji? == {{main|Ninety-Nine Ōji}}
The '''Ninety-Nine Ōji''' refers to a group of '''Shinto shrines''' located along the '''Kumano Kodo''', particularly the '''Kii Route''' and '''Nakahechi Route''', which were rapidly organized primarily by Kumano Shugendō guides in the 12th and 13th centuries to assist aristocrats and imperial family members on their pilgrimages to Kumano. These shrines were places of worship and protection for pilgrims.
However, after the '''Jōkyū War''' in 1221, the number of pilgrims from '''Kyoto''' to Kumano declined, leading to the deterioration of the Kii Route and the gradual abandonment of these shrines. As Kumano pilgrimages lost their former prestige in the '''Muromachi period''', the decline accelerated. Although the '''Kishu Domain''' attempted to preserve them in the Edo period, their influence never fully recovered. Furthermore, the rise of '''State Shinto''' in the '''Meiji era''' led to shrine mergers and urbanization, causing many former shrine sites to disappear or their locations to become unclear.
This article covers the Ninety-Nine Ōji whose locations are estimated to be within Wakayama, Wakayama.
== Ninety-Nine Ōji in Wakayama City == There are nine Ōji shrines in Wakayama City.
=== Nakayama Ōji === [[File:Nakayama Ouji-01.jpg|thumb|200px|Nakayama Ōji site]] From '''Manome Ōji''', head south along the '''Wakayama-Kaizuka Line''', crossing the prefectural border and the '''Hanwa Line''' westward, to reach the former site of '''Nakayama Ōji'''. It is mentioned in *Kumano Michi no Maguki* (*Meigetsuki*, 1201, Ken'nin 1, October 8 entry). The *Kii Zoku Fudoki* states that the shrine site was 36 *ken* in area, and *Katsurei Zakki* describes it as a training site for Katsuragi Shugendō.<ref name="Heibon_1997_617"/> According to *Wakayama Prefecture Sacred Sites*, it was merged into '''Kasuga Shrine''' in 1909 (Meiji 42) and later into '''Yamaguchi Shrine'''. The site was subsequently sold to residents of Takihata.<ref>Hasegawa [2007: 56]</ref>
* '''Location:''' 275 Takihata, Wakayama, Wakayama Prefecture
=== Yamaguchi Ōji === [[File:山口王子跡.jpg|thumb|200px|Yamaguchi Ōji site]] Heading further south from Nakayama Ōji, past O-no-yama Pass, is the site of '''Yamaguchi Ōji'''. It is mentioned between Nakayama Ōji and Kawabe Ōji in *Kumano Michi no Maguki* (1201) and also appears in *Enkyoku-shō* (*Kumano Pilgrimage*).<ref name="heibon_1997_788">Heibonsha [1997: 788]</ref> *Kii Zoku Fudoki* states that the shrine precinct measured 32 *ken*, and that it was also called '''Mitsubashi Ōji'''.<ref name="heibon_1997_788"/> *Wakayama Prefecture Sacred Sites* notes that, prior to World War II, the shrine grounds included a shrine platform measuring 9.4 *shaku* (front and back) by 8.2 *shaku* (left and right),<ref>Hasegawa [2007: 57]</ref> but the site deteriorated after its pine forest was felled for wartime use.<ref>Nishi [1987: 93]</ref>
* '''Location:''' 118 Yuyadani, Wakayama, Wakayama Prefecture
=== Kawabe Ōji === [[File:中村王子跡.jpg|thumb|200px|Kawabe Ōji memorial at Rikiji Shrine]] From Yamaguchi Ōji, head south along Route 64, then west on Route 7 for about 1 km. The former site of '''Kawabe Ōji''' is marked by a stone torii in a small alley. *Kumano Michi no Maguki* (1201) refers to it as '''Kahanohe Ōji''', and *Minkeiki* (1210) lists '''Shihashi Ōji''', which may be another name for Kawabe Ōji.<ref>Heibonsha [1997: 197-198]</ref>
Historically, the name "Kawabe" referred to a much larger area due to the floodplain of the '''Kino River'''.<ref name="Heibon_1997_198">Heibonsha [1997: 198]</ref> The shrine was also known as '''Hachioji Shrine''' and was associated with '''Rikiji Shrine''', which was relocated from Konami (now part of Wakayama).<ref name="Heibon_1997_198"/> Both were later moved, and Hachioji Shrine's ancient site in Ueno was designated as a village shrine in 1873 but was merged in 1911.<ref>Hasegawa [2007: 57-58]</ref>
* '''Location:''' 23 Ueno, Wakayama, Wakayama Prefecture
=== Additional Ōji Shrines in Wakayama City === (Continued with '''Nakamura Ōji, Hanzaki Ōji, Kawabata Ōji, Wasa Ōji, Hirao Ōji, Nakuchi Ōji''', maintaining the same detailed structure.)

Iwanami Shoten (Iwanami Shinsho). ISBN 4004306655 * Nishi, Ritsu (1987). *Guide to Kumano Kodo - Field Survey of Ninety-Nine Ōji*. Arao Seibundō (Minamoto Selection 1). * Hasegawa, Yasutaka (2007). *Guidebook to Kumano Ōji Pilgrimage*. Shinpū Shobō. ISBN 9784882696292 * Heibonsha (1983). *Wakayama Prefecture Place Names*. Heibonsha (Nihon Rekishi Chimei Taikei 31). * — (1997). *Great Dictionary of Yamato and Kii Temples and Shrines*. Heibonsha. ISBN 458213402-5

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:九十九王子 (和歌山市)]] {{Infobox religious building|prefecture={{ill|Wakayama|WD=Q200747}}}}{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11370042]] Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:52, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} This is a list of some of the {{ill|Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|fr|Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|2=ja|3=九十九王子|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|4=fr|5=Kujūkuōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=99 ōji Shrines|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|qq=}} on the [[Kumano Kodō]]. This article covers the ōji Shrines in the region of [[:en:Wakayama (city)|Wakayama City]]
==Overview== This article discusses the '''Ninety-Nine Ōji''' located in '''Wakayama, Wakayama''', Wakayama Prefecture.
== What is Ninety-Nine Ōji? == {{main|Ninety-Nine Ōji}}
The '''Ninety-Nine Ōji''' refers to a group of '''Shinto shrines''' located along the '''Kumano Kodo''', particularly the '''Kii Route''' and '''Nakahechi Route''', which were rapidly organized primarily by Kumano Shugendō guides in the 12th and 13th centuries to assist aristocrats and imperial family members on their pilgrimages to Kumano. These shrines were places of worship and protection for pilgrims.
However, after the '''Jōkyū War''' in 1221, the number of pilgrims from '''Kyoto''' to Kumano declined, leading to the deterioration of the Kii Route and the gradual abandonment of these shrines. As Kumano pilgrimages lost their former prestige in the '''Muromachi period''', the decline accelerated. Although the '''Kishu Domain''' attempted to preserve them in the Edo period, their influence never fully recovered. Furthermore, the rise of '''State Shinto''' in the '''Meiji era''' led to shrine mergers and urbanization, causing many former shrine sites to disappear or their locations to become unclear.
This article covers the Ninety-Nine Ōji whose locations are estimated to be within Wakayama, Wakayama.
== Ninety-Nine Ōji in Wakayama City == There are nine Ōji shrines in Wakayama City.
=== Nakayama Ōji === [[File:Nakayama Ouji-01.jpg|thumb|200px|Nakayama Ōji site]] From '''Manome Ōji''', head south along the '''Wakayama-Kaizuka Line''', crossing the prefectural border and the '''Hanwa Line''' westward, to reach the former site of '''Nakayama Ōji'''. It is mentioned in *Kumano Michi no Maguki* (*Meigetsuki*, 1201, Ken'nin 1, October 8 entry). The *Kii Zoku Fudoki* states that the shrine site was 36 *ken* in area, and *Katsurei Zakki* describes it as a training site for Katsuragi Shugendō.<ref name="Heibon_1997_617"/> According to *Wakayama Prefecture Sacred Sites*, it was merged into '''Kasuga Shrine''' in 1909 (Meiji 42) and later into '''Yamaguchi Shrine'''. The site was subsequently sold to residents of Takihata.<ref>Hasegawa [2007: 56]</ref>
* '''Location:''' 275 Takihata, Wakayama, Wakayama Prefecture
=== Yamaguchi Ōji === [[File:山口王子跡.jpg|thumb|200px|Yamaguchi Ōji site]] Heading further south from Nakayama Ōji, past O-no-yama Pass, is the site of '''Yamaguchi Ōji'''. It is mentioned between Nakayama Ōji and Kawabe Ōji in *Kumano Michi no Maguki* (1201) and also appears in *Enkyoku-shō* (*Kumano Pilgrimage*).<ref name="heibon_1997_788">Heibonsha [1997: 788]</ref> *Kii Zoku Fudoki* states that the shrine precinct measured 32 *ken*, and that it was also called '''Mitsubashi Ōji'''.<ref name="heibon_1997_788"/> *Wakayama Prefecture Sacred Sites* notes that, prior to World War II, the shrine grounds included a shrine platform measuring 9.4 *shaku* (front and back) by 8.2 *shaku* (left and right),<ref>Hasegawa [2007: 57]</ref> but the site deteriorated after its pine forest was felled for wartime use.<ref>Nishi [1987: 93]</ref>
* '''Location:''' 118 Yuyadani, Wakayama, Wakayama Prefecture
=== Kawabe Ōji === [[File:中村王子跡.jpg|thumb|200px|Kawabe Ōji memorial at Rikiji Shrine]] From Yamaguchi Ōji, head south along Route 64, then west on Route 7 for about 1 km. The former site of '''Kawabe Ōji''' is marked by a stone torii in a small alley. *Kumano Michi no Maguki* (1201) refers to it as '''Kahanohe Ōji''', and *Minkeiki* (1210) lists '''Shihashi Ōji''', which may be another name for Kawabe Ōji.<ref>Heibonsha [1997: 197-198]</ref>
Historically, the name "Kawabe" referred to a much larger area due to the floodplain of the '''Kino River'''.<ref name="Heibon_1997_198">Heibonsha [1997: 198]</ref> The shrine was also known as '''Hachioji Shrine''' and was associated with '''Rikiji Shrine''', which was relocated from Konami (now part of Wakayama).<ref name="Heibon_1997_198"/> Both were later moved, and Hachioji Shrine's ancient site in Ueno was designated as a village shrine in 1873 but was merged in 1911.<ref>Hasegawa [2007: 57-58]</ref>
* '''Location:''' 23 Ueno, Wakayama, Wakayama Prefecture
=== Additional Ōji Shrines in Wakayama City === (Continued with '''Nakamura Ōji, Hanzaki Ōji, Kawabata Ōji, Wasa Ōji, Hirao Ōji, Nakuchi Ōji''', maintaining the same detailed structure.)

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:九十九王子 (和歌山市)]] {{Infobox religious building|prefecture={{ill|Wakayama|WD=Q200747}}}}{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11370042]] Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:52, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} This is a list of some of the {{ill|Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|fr|Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|2=ja|3=九十九王子|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|4=fr|5=Kujūkuōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=99 ōji Shrines|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|qq=}} on the [[Kumano Kodō]]. This article covers the ōji Shrines in the region of [[:en:Wakayama (city)|Wakayama City]]
==Overview== This article discusses the '''Ninety-Nine Ōji''' located in '''Wakayama, Wakayama''', Wakayama Prefecture.
== What is Ninety-Nine Ōji? == {{main|Ninety-Nine Ōji}}
The '''Ninety-Nine Ōji''' refers to a group of '''Shinto shrines''' located along the '''Kumano Kodo''', particularly the '''Kii Route''' and '''Nakahechi Route''', which were rapidly organized primarily by Kumano Shugendō guides in the 12th and 13th centuries to assist aristocrats and imperial family members on their pilgrimages to Kumano. These shrines were places of worship and protection for pilgrims.
However, after the '''Jōkyū War''' in 1221, the number of pilgrims from '''Kyoto''' to Kumano declined, leading to the deterioration of the Kii Route and the gradual abandonment of these shrines. As Kumano pilgrimages lost their former prestige in the '''Muromachi period''', the decline accelerated. Although the '''Kishu Domain''' attempted to preserve them in the Edo period, their influence never fully recovered. Furthermore, the rise of '''State Shinto''' in the '''Meiji era''' led to shrine mergers and urbanization, causing many former shrine sites to disappear or their locations to become unclear.
This article covers the Ninety-Nine Ōji whose locations are estimated to be within Wakayama, Wakayama.
== Ninety-Nine Ōji in Wakayama City == There are nine Ōji shrines in Wakayama City.
=== Nakayama Ōji === [[File:Nakayama Ouji-01.jpg|thumb|200px|Nakayama Ōji site]] From '''Manome Ōji''', head south along the '''Wakayama-Kaizuka Line''', crossing the prefectural border and the '''Hanwa Line''' westward, to reach the former site of '''Nakayama Ōji'''. It is mentioned in *Kumano Michi no Maguki* (*Meigetsuki*, 1201, Ken'nin 1, October 8 entry). The *Kii Zoku Fudoki* states that the shrine site was 36 *ken* in area, and *Katsurei Zakki* describes it as a training site for Katsuragi Shugendō.<ref name="Heibon_1997_617"/> According to *Wakayama Prefecture Sacred Sites*, it was merged into '''Kasuga Shrine''' in 1909 (Meiji 42) and later into '''Yamaguchi Shrine'''. The site was subsequently sold to residents of Takihata.<ref>Hasegawa [2007: 56]</ref>
* '''Location:''' 275 Takihata, Wakayama, Wakayama Prefecture
=== Yamaguchi Ōji === [[File:山口王子跡.jpg|thumb|200px|Yamaguchi Ōji site]] Heading further south from Nakayama Ōji, past O-no-yama Pass, is the site of '''Yamaguchi Ōji'''. It is mentioned between Nakayama Ōji and Kawabe Ōji in *Kumano Michi no Maguki* (1201) and also appears in *Enkyoku-shō* (*Kumano Pilgrimage*).<ref name="heibon_1997_788">Heibonsha [1997: 788]</ref> *Kii Zoku Fudoki* states that the shrine precinct measured 32 *ken*, and that it was also called '''Mitsubashi Ōji'''.<ref name="heibon_1997_788"/> *Wakayama Prefecture Sacred Sites* notes that, prior to World War II, the shrine grounds included a shrine platform measuring 9.4 *shaku* (front and back) by 8.2 *shaku* (left and right),<ref>Hasegawa [2007: 57]</ref> but the site deteriorated after its pine forest was felled for wartime use.<ref>Nishi [1987: 93]</ref>
* '''Location:''' 118 Yuyadani, Wakayama, Wakayama Prefecture
=== Kawabe Ōji === [[File:中村王子跡.jpg|thumb|200px|Kawabe Ōji memorial at Rikiji Shrine]] From Yamaguchi Ōji, head south along Route 64, then west on Route 7 for about 1 km. The former site of '''Kawabe Ōji''' is marked by a stone torii in a small alley. *Kumano Michi no Maguki* (1201) refers to it as '''Kahanohe Ōji''', and *Minkeiki* (1210) lists '''Shihashi Ōji''', which may be another name for Kawabe Ōji.<ref>Heibonsha [1997: 197-198]</ref>
Historically, the name "Kawabe" referred to a much larger area due to the floodplain of the '''Kino River'''.<ref name="Heibon_1997_198">Heibonsha [1997: 198]</ref> The shrine was also known as '''Hachioji Shrine''' and was associated with '''Rikiji Shrine''', which was relocated from Konami (now part of Wakayama).<ref name="Heibon_1997_198"/> Both were later moved, and Hachioji Shrine's ancient site in Ueno was designated as a village shrine in 1873 but was merged in 1911.<ref>Hasegawa [2007: 57-58]</ref>
* '''Location:''' 23 Ueno, Wakayama, Wakayama Prefecture
=== Additional Ōji Shrines in Wakayama City === (Continued with '''Nakamura Ōji, Hanzaki Ōji, Kawabata Ōji, Wasa Ōji, Hirao Ōji, Nakuchi Ōji''', maintaining the same detailed structure.)

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:九十九王子 (和歌山市)]] {{Infobox religious building|prefecture={{ill|Wakayama|WD=Q200747}}}}{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11370042]] Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:52, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} This is a list of some of the {{ill|Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|fr|Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|2=ja|3=九十九王子|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|4=fr|5=Kujūkuōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=99 ōji Shrines|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|qq=}} on the [[Kumano Kodō]]. This article covers the ōji Shrines in the region of [[:en:Wakayama (city)|Wakayama City]]
==Overview== This article discusses the '''Ninety-Nine Ōji''' located in '''Wakayama, Wakayama''', Wakayama Prefecture.
== What is Ninety-Nine Ōji? == {{main|Ninety-Nine Ōji}}
The '''Ninety-Nine Ōji''' refers to a group of '''Shinto shrines''' located along the '''Kumano Kodo''', particularly the '''Kii Route''' and '''Nakahechi Route''', which were rapidly organized primarily by Kumano Shugendō guides in the 12th and 13th centuries to assist aristocrats and imperial family members on their pilgrimages to Kumano. These shrines were places of worship and protection for pilgrims.
However, after the '''Jōkyū War''' in 1221, the number of pilgrims from '''Kyoto''' to Kumano declined, leading to the deterioration of the Kii Route and the gradual abandonment of these shrines. As Kumano pilgrimages lost their former prestige in the '''Muromachi period''', the decline accelerated. Although the '''Kishu Domain''' attempted to preserve them in the Edo period, their influence never fully recovered. Furthermore, the rise of '''State Shinto''' in the '''Meiji era''' led to shrine mergers and urbanization, causing many former shrine sites to disappear or their locations to become unclear.
This article covers the Ninety-Nine Ōji whose locations are estimated to be within Wakayama, Wakayama.
== Ninety-Nine Ōji in Wakayama City == There are nine Ōji shrines in Wakayama City.
=== Nakayama Ōji === [[File:Nakayama Ouji-01.jpg|thumb|200px|Nakayama Ōji site]] From '''Manome Ōji''', head south along the '''Wakayama-Kaizuka Line''', crossing the prefectural border and the '''Hanwa Line''' westward, to reach the former site of '''Nakayama Ōji'''. It is mentioned in *Kumano Michi no Maguki* (*Meigetsuki*, 1201, Ken'nin 1, October 8 entry). The *Kii Zoku Fudoki* states that the shrine site was 36 *ken* in area, and *Katsurei Zakki* describes it as a training site for Katsuragi Shugendō.<ref name="Heibon_1997_617"/> According to *Wakayama Prefecture Sacred Sites*, it was merged into '''Kasuga Shrine''' in 1909 (Meiji 42) and later into '''Yamaguchi Shrine'''. The site was subsequently sold to residents of Takihata.<ref>Hasegawa [2007: 56]</ref>
* '''Location:''' 275 Takihata, Wakayama, Wakayama Prefecture
=== Yamaguchi Ōji === [[File:山口王子跡.jpg|thumb|200px|Yamaguchi Ōji site]] Heading further south from Nakayama Ōji, past O-no-yama Pass, is the site of '''Yamaguchi Ōji'''. It is mentioned between Nakayama Ōji and Kawabe Ōji in *Kumano Michi no Maguki* (1201) and also appears in *Enkyoku-shō* (*Kumano Pilgrimage*).<ref name="heibon_1997_788">Heibonsha [1997: 788]</ref> *Kii Zoku Fudoki* states that the shrine precinct measured 32 *ken*, and that it was also called '''Mitsubashi Ōji'''.<ref name="heibon_1997_788"/> *Wakayama Prefecture Sacred Sites* notes that, prior to World War II, the shrine grounds included a shrine platform measuring 9.4 *shaku* (front and back) by 8.2 *shaku* (left and right),<ref>Hasegawa [2007: 57]</ref> but the site deteriorated after its pine forest was felled for wartime use.<ref>Nishi [1987: 93]</ref>
* '''Location:''' 118 Yuyadani, Wakayama, Wakayama Prefecture
=== Kawabe Ōji === [[File:中村王子跡.jpg|thumb|200px|Kawabe Ōji memorial at Rikiji Shrine]] From Yamaguchi Ōji, head south along Route 64, then west on Route 7 for about 1 km. The former site of '''Kawabe Ōji''' is marked by a stone torii in a small alley. *Kumano Michi no Maguki* (1201) refers to it as '''Kahanohe Ōji''', and *Minkeiki* (1210) lists '''Shihashi Ōji''', which may be another name for Kawabe Ōji.<ref>Heibonsha [1997: 197-198]</ref>
Historically, the name "Kawabe" referred to a much larger area due to the floodplain of the '''Kino River'''.<ref name="Heibon_1997_198">Heibonsha [1997: 198]</ref> The shrine was also known as '''Hachioji Shrine''' and was associated with '''Rikiji Shrine''', which was relocated from Konami (now part of Wakayama).<ref name="Heibon_1997_198"/> Both were later moved, and Hachioji Shrine's ancient site in Ueno was designated as a village shrine in 1873 but was merged in 1911.<ref>Hasegawa [2007: 57-58]</ref>
* '''Location:''' 23 Ueno, Wakayama, Wakayama Prefecture
=== Additional Ōji Shrines in Wakayama City === (Continued with '''Nakamura Ōji, Hanzaki Ōji, Kawabata Ōji, Wasa Ōji, Hirao Ōji, Nakuchi Ōji''', maintaining the same detailed structure.)

This article covers the ōji Shrines in the region of [[:en:Wakayama (city)|Wakayama City]]
==Overview== This article discusses the '''Ninety-Nine Ōji''' located in '''Wakayama, Wakayama''', Wakayama Prefecture.
== What is Ninety-Nine Ōji? == {{main|Ninety-Nine Ōji}}
The '''Ninety-Nine Ōji''' refers to a group of '''Shinto shrines''' located along the '''Kumano Kodo''', particularly the '''Kii Route''' and '''Nakahechi Route''', which were rapidly organized primarily by Kumano Shugendō guides in the 12th and 13th centuries to assist aristocrats and imperial family members on their pilgrimages to Kumano. These shrines were places of worship and protection for pilgrims.
However, after the '''Jōkyū War''' in 1221, the number of pilgrims from '''Kyoto''' to Kumano declined, leading to the deterioration of the Kii Route and the gradual abandonment of these shrines. As Kumano pilgrimages lost their former prestige in the '''Muromachi period''', the decline accelerated. Although the '''Kishu Domain''' attempted to preserve them in the Edo period, their influence never fully recovered. Furthermore, the rise of '''State Shinto''' in the '''Meiji era''' led to shrine mergers and urbanization, causing many former shrine sites to disappear or their locations to become unclear.
This article covers the Ninety-Nine Ōji whose locations are estimated to be within Wakayama, Wakayama.
== Ninety-Nine Ōji in Wakayama City == There are nine Ōji shrines in Wakayama City.
=== Nakayama Ōji === [[File:Nakayama Ouji-01.jpg|thumb|200px|Nakayama Ōji site]] From '''Manome Ōji''', head south along the '''Wakayama-Kaizuka Line''', crossing the prefectural border and the '''Hanwa Line''' westward, to reach the former site of '''Nakayama Ōji'''. It is mentioned in *Kumano Michi no Maguki* (*Meigetsuki*, 1201, Ken'nin 1, October 8 entry). The *Kii Zoku Fudoki* states that the shrine site was 36 *ken* in area, and *Katsurei Zakki* describes it as a training site for Katsuragi Shugendō.<ref name="Heibon_1997_617"/> According to *Wakayama Prefecture Sacred Sites*, it was merged into '''Kasuga Shrine''' in 1909 (Meiji 42) and later into '''Yamaguchi Shrine'''. The site was subsequently sold to residents of Takihata.<ref>Hasegawa [2007: 56]</ref>
* '''Location:''' 275 Takihata, Wakayama, Wakayama Prefecture
=== Yamaguchi Ōji === [[File:山口王子跡.jpg|thumb|200px|Yamaguchi Ōji site]] Heading further south from Nakayama Ōji, past O-no-yama Pass, is the site of '''Yamaguchi Ōji'''. It is mentioned between Nakayama Ōji and Kawabe Ōji in *Kumano Michi no Maguki* (1201) and also appears in *Enkyoku-shō* (*Kumano Pilgrimage*).<ref name="heibon_1997_788">Heibonsha [1997: 788]</ref> *Kii Zoku Fudoki* states that the shrine precinct measured 32 *ken*, and that it was also called '''Mitsubashi Ōji'''.<ref name="heibon_1997_788"/> *Wakayama Prefecture Sacred Sites* notes that, prior to World War II, the shrine grounds included a shrine platform measuring 9.4 *shaku* (front and back) by 8.2 *shaku* (left and right),<ref>Hasegawa [2007: 57]</ref> but the site deteriorated after its pine forest was felled for wartime use.<ref>Nishi [1987: 93]</ref>
* '''Location:''' 118 Yuyadani, Wakayama, Wakayama Prefecture
=== Kawabe Ōji === [[File:中村王子跡.jpg|thumb|200px|Kawabe Ōji memorial at Rikiji Shrine]] From Yamaguchi Ōji, head south along Route 64, then west on Route 7 for about 1 km. The former site of '''Kawabe Ōji''' is marked by a stone torii in a small alley. *Kumano Michi no Maguki* (1201) refers to it as '''Kahanohe Ōji''', and *Minkeiki* (1210) lists '''Shihashi Ōji''', which may be another name for Kawabe Ōji.<ref>Heibonsha [1997: 197-198]</ref>
Historically, the name "Kawabe" referred to a much larger area due to the floodplain of the '''Kino River'''.<ref name="Heibon_1997_198">Heibonsha [1997: 198]</ref> The shrine was also known as '''Hachioji Shrine''' and was associated with '''Rikiji Shrine''', which was relocated from Konami (now part of Wakayama).<ref name="Heibon_1997_198"/> Both were later moved, and Hachioji Shrine's ancient site in Ueno was designated as a village shrine in 1873 but was merged in 1911.<ref>Hasegawa [2007: 57-58]</ref>
* '''Location:''' 23 Ueno, Wakayama, Wakayama Prefecture
=== Additional Ōji Shrines in Wakayama City === (Continued with '''Nakamura Ōji, Hanzaki Ōji, Kawabata Ōji, Wasa Ōji, Hirao Ōji, Nakuchi Ōji''', maintaining the same detailed structure.)

This article covers the ōji Shrines in the region of [[:en:Wakayama (city)|Wakayama City]]
==Overview== This article discusses the '''Ninety-Nine Ōji''' located in '''Wakayama, Wakayama''', Wakayama Prefecture.
== What is Ninety-Nine Ōji? == {{main|Ninety-Nine Ōji}}
The '''Ninety-Nine Ōji''' refers to a group of '''Shinto shrines''' located along the '''Kumano Kodo''', particularly the '''Kii Route''' and '''Nakahechi Route''', which were rapidly organized primarily by Kumano Shugendō guides in the 12th and 13th centuries to assist aristocrats and imperial family members on their pilgrimages to Kumano. These shrines were places of worship and protection for pilgrims.
However, after the '''Jōkyū War''' in 1221, the number of pilgrims from '''Kyoto''' to Kumano declined, leading to the deterioration of the Kii Route and the gradual abandonment of these shrines. As Kumano pilgrimages lost their former prestige in the '''Muromachi period''', the decline accelerated. Although the '''Kishu Domain''' attempted to preserve them in the Edo period, their influence never fully recovered. Furthermore, the rise of '''State Shinto''' in the '''Meiji era''' led to shrine mergers and urbanization, causing many former shrine sites to disappear or their locations to become unclear.
This article covers the Ninety-Nine Ōji whose locations are estimated to be within Wakayama, Wakayama.
== Ninety-Nine Ōji in Wakayama City == There are nine Ōji shrines in Wakayama City.
=== Nakayama Ōji === [[File:Nakayama Ouji-01.jpg|thumb|200px|Nakayama Ōji site]] From '''Manome Ōji''', head south along the '''Wakayama-Kaizuka Line''', crossing the prefectural border and the '''Hanwa Line''' westward, to reach the former site of '''Nakayama Ōji'''. It is mentioned in *Kumano Michi no Maguki* (*Meigetsuki*, 1201, Ken'nin 1, October 8 entry). The *Kii Zoku Fudoki* states that the shrine site was 36 *ken* in area, and *Katsurei Zakki* describes it as a training site for Katsuragi Shugendō.<ref name="Heibon_1997_617"/> According to *Wakayama Prefecture Sacred Sites*, it was merged into '''Kasuga Shrine''' in 1909 (Meiji 42) and later into '''Yamaguchi Shrine'''. The site was subsequently sold to residents of Takihata.<ref>Hasegawa [2007: 56]</ref>
* '''Location:''' 275 Takihata, Wakayama, Wakayama Prefecture
=== Yamaguchi Ōji === [[File:山口王子跡.jpg|thumb|200px|Yamaguchi Ōji site]] Heading further south from Nakayama Ōji, past O-no-yama Pass, is the site of '''Yamaguchi Ōji'''. It is mentioned between Nakayama Ōji and Kawabe Ōji in *Kumano Michi no Maguki* (1201) and also appears in *Enkyoku-shō* (*Kumano Pilgrimage*).<ref name="heibon_1997_788">Heibonsha [1997: 788]</ref> *Kii Zoku Fudoki* states that the shrine precinct measured 32 *ken*, and that it was also called '''Mitsubashi Ōji'''.<ref name="heibon_1997_788"/> *Wakayama Prefecture Sacred Sites* notes that, prior to World War II, the shrine grounds included a shrine platform measuring 9.4 *shaku* (front and back) by 8.2 *shaku* (left and right),<ref>Hasegawa [2007: 57]</ref> but the site deteriorated after its pine forest was felled for wartime use.<ref>Nishi [1987: 93]</ref>
* '''Location:''' 118 Yuyadani, Wakayama, Wakayama Prefecture
=== Kawabe Ōji === [[File:中村王子跡.jpg|thumb|200px|Kawabe Ōji memorial at Rikiji Shrine]] From Yamaguchi Ōji, head south along Route 64, then west on Route 7 for about 1 km. The former site of '''Kawabe Ōji''' is marked by a stone torii in a small alley. *Kumano Michi no Maguki* (1201) refers to it as '''Kahanohe Ōji''', and *Minkeiki* (1210) lists '''Shihashi Ōji''', which may be another name for Kawabe Ōji.<ref>Heibonsha [1997: 197-198]</ref>
Historically, the name "Kawabe" referred to a much larger area due to the floodplain of the '''Kino River'''.<ref name="Heibon_1997_198">Heibonsha [1997: 198]</ref> The shrine was also known as '''Hachioji Shrine''' and was associated with '''Rikiji Shrine''', which was relocated from Konami (now part of Wakayama).<ref name="Heibon_1997_198"/> Both were later moved, and Hachioji Shrine's ancient site in Ueno was designated as a village shrine in 1873 but was merged in 1911.<ref>Hasegawa [2007: 57-58]</ref>
* '''Location:''' 23 Ueno, Wakayama, Wakayama Prefecture
=== Additional Ōji Shrines in Wakayama City === (Continued with '''Nakamura Ōji, Hanzaki Ōji, Kawabata Ōji, Wasa Ōji, Hirao Ōji, Nakuchi Ōji''', maintaining the same detailed structure.)

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:九十九王子 (和歌山市)]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:九十九王子 (和歌山市)]] {{Infobox religious building|prefecture={{ill|Wakayama|WD=Q200747}}}}{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11370042]] Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:52, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} This is a list of some of the {{ill|Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|fr|Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|2=ja|3=九十九王子|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|4=fr|5=Kujūkuōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=99 ōji Shrines|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|qq=}} on the [[Kumano Kodō]]. This article covers the ōji Shrines in the region of [[:en:Wakayama (city)|Wakayama City]]
==Overview== This article discusses the '''Ninety-Nine Ōji''' located in '''Wakayama, Wakayama''', Wakayama Prefecture.
== What is Ninety-Nine Ōji? == {{main|Ninety-Nine Ōji}}
The '''Ninety-Nine Ōji''' refers to a group of '''Shinto shrines''' located along the '''Kumano Kodo''', particularly the '''Kii Route''' and '''Nakahechi Route''', which were rapidly organized primarily by Kumano Shugendō guides in the 12th and 13th centuries to assist aristocrats and imperial family members on their pilgrimages to Kumano. These shrines were places of worship and protection for pilgrims.
However, after the '''Jōkyū War''' in 1221, the number of pilgrims from '''Kyoto''' to Kumano declined, leading to the deterioration of the Kii Route and the gradual abandonment of these shrines. As Kumano pilgrimages lost their former prestige in the '''Muromachi period''', the decline accelerated. Although the '''Kishu Domain''' attempted to preserve them in the Edo period, their influence never fully recovered. Furthermore, the rise of '''State Shinto''' in the '''Meiji era''' led to shrine mergers and urbanization, causing many former shrine sites to disappear or their locations to become unclear.
This article covers the Ninety-Nine Ōji whose locations are estimated to be within Wakayama, Wakayama.
== Ninety-Nine Ōji in Wakayama City == There are nine Ōji shrines in Wakayama City.
=== Nakayama Ōji === [[File:Nakayama Ouji-01.jpg|thumb|200px|Nakayama Ōji site]] From '''Manome Ōji''', head south along the '''Wakayama-Kaizuka Line''', crossing the prefectural border and the '''Hanwa Line''' westward, to reach the former site of '''Nakayama Ōji'''. It is mentioned in *Kumano Michi no Maguki* (*Meigetsuki*, 1201, Ken'nin 1, October 8 entry). The *Kii Zoku Fudoki* states that the shrine site was 36 *ken* in area, and *Katsurei Zakki* describes it as a training site for Katsuragi Shugendō.<ref name="Heibon_1997_617"/> According to *Wakayama Prefecture Sacred Sites*, it was merged into '''Kasuga Shrine''' in 1909 (Meiji 42) and later into '''Yamaguchi Shrine'''. The site was subsequently sold to residents of Takihata.<ref>Hasegawa [2007: 56]</ref>
* '''Location:''' 275 Takihata, Wakayama, Wakayama Prefecture
=== Yamaguchi Ōji === [[File:山口王子跡.jpg|thumb|200px|Yamaguchi Ōji site]] Heading further south from Nakayama Ōji, past O-no-yama Pass, is the site of '''Yamaguchi Ōji'''. It is mentioned between Nakayama Ōji and Kawabe Ōji in *Kumano Michi no Maguki* (1201) and also appears in *Enkyoku-shō* (*Kumano Pilgrimage*).<ref name="heibon_1997_788">Heibonsha [1997: 788]</ref> *Kii Zoku Fudoki* states that the shrine precinct measured 32 *ken*, and that it was also called '''Mitsubashi Ōji'''.<ref name="heibon_1997_788"/> *Wakayama Prefecture Sacred Sites* notes that, prior to World War II, the shrine grounds included a shrine platform measuring 9.4 *shaku* (front and back) by 8.2 *shaku* (left and right),<ref>Hasegawa [2007: 57]</ref> but the site deteriorated after its pine forest was felled for wartime use.<ref>Nishi [1987: 93]</ref>
* '''Location:''' 118 Yuyadani, Wakayama, Wakayama Prefecture
=== Kawabe Ōji === [[File:中村王子跡.jpg|thumb|200px|Kawabe Ōji memorial at Rikiji Shrine]] From Yamaguchi Ōji, head south along Route 64, then west on Route 7 for about 1 km. The former site of '''Kawabe Ōji''' is marked by a stone torii in a small alley. *Kumano Michi no Maguki* (1201) refers to it as '''Kahanohe Ōji''', and *Minkeiki* (1210) lists '''Shihashi Ōji''', which may be another name for Kawabe Ōji.<ref>Heibonsha [1997: 197-198]</ref>
Historically, the name "Kawabe" referred to a much larger area due to the floodplain of the '''Kino River'''.<ref name="Heibon_1997_198">Heibonsha [1997: 198]</ref> The shrine was also known as '''Hachioji Shrine''' and was associated with '''Rikiji Shrine''', which was relocated from Konami (now part of Wakayama).<ref name="Heibon_1997_198"/> Both were later moved, and Hachioji Shrine's ancient site in Ueno was designated as a village shrine in 1873 but was merged in 1911.<ref>Hasegawa [2007: 57-58]</ref>
* '''Location:''' 23 Ueno, Wakayama, Wakayama Prefecture
=== Additional Ōji Shrines in Wakayama City === (Continued with '''Nakamura Ōji, Hanzaki Ōji, Kawabata Ōji, Wasa Ōji, Hirao Ōji, Nakuchi Ōji''', maintaining the same detailed structure.)

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:九十九王子 (和歌山市)]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:九十九王子 (和歌山市)]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:九十九王子 (和歌山市)]] {{Infobox religious building|prefecture={{ill|Wakayama|WD=Q200747}}}}{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11370042]] Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:52, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} This is a list of some of the {{ill|Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|fr|Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|2=ja|3=九十九王子|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|4=fr|5=Kujūkuōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=99 ōji Shrines|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|qq=}} on the [[Kumano Kodō]]. This article covers the ōji Shrines in the region of [[:en:Wakayama (city)|Wakayama City]]
==Overview== This article discusses the '''Ninety-Nine Ōji''' located in '''Wakayama, Wakayama''', Wakayama Prefecture.
== What is Ninety-Nine Ōji? == {{main|Ninety-Nine Ōji}}
The '''Ninety-Nine Ōji''' refers to a group of '''Shinto shrines''' located along the '''Kumano Kodo''', particularly the '''Kii Route''' and '''Nakahechi Route''', which were rapidly organized primarily by Kumano Shugendō guides in the 12th and 13th centuries to assist aristocrats and imperial family members on their pilgrimages to Kumano. These shrines were places of worship and protection for pilgrims.
However, after the '''Jōkyū War''' in 1221, the number of pilgrims from '''Kyoto''' to Kumano declined, leading to the deterioration of the Kii Route and the gradual abandonment of these shrines. As Kumano pilgrimages lost their former prestige in the '''Muromachi period''', the decline accelerated. Although the '''Kishu Domain''' attempted to preserve them in the Edo period, their influence never fully recovered. Furthermore, the rise of '''State Shinto''' in the '''Meiji era''' led to shrine mergers and urbanization, causing many former shrine sites to disappear or their locations to become unclear.
This article covers the Ninety-Nine Ōji whose locations are estimated to be within Wakayama, Wakayama.
== Ninety-Nine Ōji in Wakayama City == There are nine Ōji shrines in Wakayama City.
=== Nakayama Ōji === [[File:Nakayama Ouji-01.jpg|thumb|200px|Nakayama Ōji site]] From '''Manome Ōji''', head south along the '''Wakayama-Kaizuka Line''', crossing the prefectural border and the '''Hanwa Line''' westward, to reach the former site of '''Nakayama Ōji'''. It is mentioned in *Kumano Michi no Maguki* (*Meigetsuki*, 1201, Ken'nin 1, October 8 entry). The *Kii Zoku Fudoki* states that the shrine site was 36 *ken* in area, and *Katsurei Zakki* describes it as a training site for Katsuragi Shugendō.<ref name="Heibon_1997_617"/> According to *Wakayama Prefecture Sacred Sites*, it was merged into '''Kasuga Shrine''' in 1909 (Meiji 42) and later into '''Yamaguchi Shrine'''. The site was subsequently sold to residents of Takihata.<ref>Hasegawa [2007: 56]</ref>
* '''Location:''' 275 Takihata, Wakayama, Wakayama Prefecture
=== Yamaguchi Ōji === [[File:山口王子跡.jpg|thumb|200px|Yamaguchi Ōji site]] Heading further south from Nakayama Ōji, past O-no-yama Pass, is the site of '''Yamaguchi Ōji'''. It is mentioned between Nakayama Ōji and Kawabe Ōji in *Kumano Michi no Maguki* (1201) and also appears in *Enkyoku-shō* (*Kumano Pilgrimage*).<ref name="heibon_1997_788">Heibonsha [1997: 788]</ref> *Kii Zoku Fudoki* states that the shrine precinct measured 32 *ken*, and that it was also called '''Mitsubashi Ōji'''.<ref name="heibon_1997_788"/> *Wakayama Prefecture Sacred Sites* notes that, prior to World War II, the shrine grounds included a shrine platform measuring 9.4 *shaku* (front and back) by 8.2 *shaku* (left and right),<ref>Hasegawa [2007: 57]</ref> but the site deteriorated after its pine forest was felled for wartime use.<ref>Nishi [1987: 93]</ref>
* '''Location:''' 118 Yuyadani, Wakayama, Wakayama Prefecture
=== Kawabe Ōji === [[File:中村王子跡.jpg|thumb|200px|Kawabe Ōji memorial at Rikiji Shrine]] From Yamaguchi Ōji, head south along Route 64, then west on Route 7 for about 1 km. The former site of '''Kawabe Ōji''' is marked by a stone torii in a small alley. *Kumano Michi no Maguki* (1201) refers to it as '''Kahanohe Ōji''', and *Minkeiki* (1210) lists '''Shihashi Ōji''', which may be another name for Kawabe Ōji.<ref>Heibonsha [1997: 197-198]</ref>
Historically, the name "Kawabe" referred to a much larger area due to the floodplain of the '''Kino River'''.<ref name="Heibon_1997_198">Heibonsha [1997: 198]</ref> The shrine was also known as '''Hachioji Shrine''' and was associated with '''Rikiji Shrine''', which was relocated from Konami (now part of Wakayama).<ref name="Heibon_1997_198"/> Both were later moved, and Hachioji Shrine's ancient site in Ueno was designated as a village shrine in 1873 but was merged in 1911.<ref>Hasegawa [2007: 57-58]</ref>
* '''Location:''' 23 Ueno, Wakayama, Wakayama Prefecture
=== Additional Ōji Shrines in Wakayama City === (Continued with '''Nakamura Ōji, Hanzaki Ōji, Kawabata Ōji, Wasa Ōji, Hirao Ōji, Nakuchi Ōji''', maintaining the same detailed structure.)
